Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order Advancing The Current Trial Date With Preference; And Extending Discovery Cutoff
Off Calendar Sep-17-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: On Asbestos Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, September 16, 2025, in Department 304, Line 1.
No appearances, not contested, The Court adopts its tentative ruling as follows:
Plaintiff's Motion for Order Advancing the Current Trial Date with Preference, and Extending Discovery Cutoff [Second Motion] is GRANTED under C.C.P. 36(a). Opposition filed by Intervenors for Defendant D/C Distribution Corporation. Reply filed.
Intervenors argue that the Declaration of Dr. Sei Lee ("Dr. Lee") lacks foundation in regard to the requirements of Code of Civil Procedure 36(a). (Opposition at p. 3.) The Court disagrees. The fact that the declaration from Dr. Lee is dated September 30, 2023, does not undermine its relevance or reliability. Intervenors cite to no authority requiring the declaration to be contemporaneous with the filing of the motion. (See Opposition at pp. 4-5.) In addition, Intervenors did not provide any contradictory medical opinion, such as a declaration from a licensed physician or a medical doctor, disputing Dr. Lee's opinion. Furthermore, Intervenors are not physicians qualified to opine on the validity of Dr. Lee's opinions. As this Court has previously stated, unsupported speculation about a plaintiff's health is not a valid basis to oppose a preference motion grounded in admissible medical evidence.
Even if there were no such evidence, it is clear that Mr. Rodrick's health is declining and his ability to participate in this litigation is in jeopardy. (See Declaration of David Donadio at 3 and 5.) Mr. Rodrick, of course, has a substantial interest in the action as a whole and his health is such that preference is necessary to prevent prejudicing his interest in the litigation. (Code Civ. Proc. 36(a).) Thus, "where a party meets the requisite standard for calendar preference under [Code of Civil Procedure section 36] subdivision (a), preference must be granted. No weighing of interests is involved." (Fox v. Superior Court (2018) 21 Cal.App.5th 529, 535; see also, e.g., Miller v. Superior Court (1990) 221 Cal.App.3d 1200, 1204 [statute "grants a mandatory and absolute right to trial preference"]; Swaithes v. Superior Court (1989) 212 Cal.App.3d 1082, 1085 [trial court "has no power to balance the different interests of opposing litigants in applying the provision"]; Koch-Ash v. Superior Court (1986) 180 Cal.App.3d 689, 694 [section 36(a) "must be deemed to be mandatory and absolute" and "no discretion is left to trial courts."].)
1. The trial date is advanced to January 5, 2026, at 11:15 a.m. in Department 206.
a. Last day pursuant to C.C.P. section 36(f) is January 14, 2026.
b. The parties shall follow the California Rules of Court, San Francisco Local Rules, and Local Rule 20.
2. The last day for hearing summary judgment/adjudication motions is December 30, 2025.
a. Summary judgment/adjudication motions shall be brought on regular notice pursuant to the relevant provisions of the C.C.P., unless the parties stipulate otherwise.
b. Before a party files and serves a summary judgment/adjudication motion, it must contact the clerk to make a reservation.
c. The Court allows a maximum of four summary judgment/adjudication motions per day to be calendared, unless good cause is found to exceed this number. Contact the clerk to schedule a good cause hearing.
3. Time to respond to written discovery not yet served is shortened to 20 days.
a. For written discovery that has already been served, responses are due within 20 days of this hearing or by the date determined by the C.C.P., whichever is earlier.
b. Any issue/dispute that requires meet and confer, shall occur in person or via telephone, not by email or letter.
4. Electronic service is considered the equivalent of personal service.
5. The fact discovery cut-off date is December 19, 2025.
6. The expert discovery cut-off date is January 2, 2026.

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Dec-14-2023 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
Not contested, no appearances, and tentative ruling adopted: Defendant Cal-Steam, Inc.'s Motion for Summary Judgment or, in the Alternative, Summary Adjudication is DENIED. Opposition filed by Plaintiffs. Reply filed by Defendant. The Court declines to find that the motion is procedurally defective. Defendant fails to sustain its initial burden of showing Plaintiffs do not possess and cannot reasonably obtain needed evidence of asbestos exposure and causation. (Aguilar v. Atl. Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826, 855.)

Melvin Rodrick stated that he "installed asbestos-containing GARLOCK gaskets and worked with asbestos-cement pipe, plumber's putty, gaskets, and other asbestos-containing materials that were supplied by CAL STEAM, INC. in Santa Rosa, California." (Defendant's Index of Exhibits, Ex. C [Plaintiffs' Verified Responses to Defendant's First Set of Special Interrogatories] at 3:4-6.) He recalled "a closed 18-wheeler . . . that had Cal-Steam's name on the side of it." (Defendant's Index of Exhibits, Ex. F [Portions of Depo. of Melvin Rodrick, Feb. 22 and 23, 2023] at 197:12-18.) He recalled seeing the 18-wheeler at "not other than probably at the shop - shops. One of them." (Id. at 197:22-25.) He identified Jim Beasley as a witness still alive today that would have more information regarding Defendant. (See id. at 200:10-16.)

The Court cannot infer Plaintiffs have no case against Defendant. (See Weber v. John Crane, Inc. (2006) 143 Cal.App.4th 1433, 1439 [stating that a "negative response to [a] question simply does not create an inference either of nonexposure or of the inability to prove exposure by some other means"].) Defendant fails to foreclose that Plaintiffs may yet prove asbestos exposure and causation attributed to Defendant at trial. (See Scheiding v. Dinwiddie Const. Co. (1999) 69 Cal.App.4th 64, 81.) As such, the Court declines to rule on Defendant's immaterial objections to Plaintiffs' opposing evidence. (See Code Civ. Proc., sec. 437c, subd. (q).)

Finally, Defendant repeats the same eight undisputed material facts for each of the three causes of action it contends must be summarily adjudicated in its favor. The Court denies the motion for summary adjudication in its entirety for the same reasons as above. Order signed in open court. Judge: Samuel K. Feng, Clerk: A. Mok. Not reported. (503/SKF)
